hero-title: Researcher Path: Going Deeper Into How AI and People Think Together
Researcher Path: A Deep Look at the Theory Behind Augmented Intelligence
Researcher Path: Deep Dive into Augmented Intelligence Theory
Researcher Path: The Theory of Augmented Intelligence
lead: You already know the basics. You already know how to use the everyday tools. So now we can slow down and look closer. This path looks at the science of how the mind works, the ideas behind good design, and the research about what people feel and need. All of that sits underneath augmented intelligence, and we will take our time with it.

You have the foundations, and you have the practical frameworks. Now it is time to go deeper. This path explores the cognitive science, the ideas behind good interaction design, and the psychological research that all sit underneath augmented intelligence.

You have the foundations. You have the practical frameworks. Now go deeper. This path explores the cognitive science, interaction design theory, and psychological research that underpin augmented intelligence.
Foundations done, frameworks applied — now go deeper. This path covers the cognitive science, interaction-design theory, and psychological research underpinning augmented intelligence.
Foundations and frameworks assumed. This path: the cognitive science, interaction-design theory, and psychology underpinning augmented intelligence.
who-1: This path is made for people who really want to dig in: researchers, academics, and people who have been using these tools for a long time. The Beginner Path showed you what augmented intelligence is. The Practitioner Path showed you how to use it. This path is different. It shows you why it works the way it does.

This path is for researchers, academics, and advanced practitioners who want to understand the theory behind human-AI collaboration more deeply. The Beginner Path taught you what augmented intelligence is, and the Practitioner Path taught you how to apply it. This path teaches you why it works the way it does.

This path is for researchers, academics, and advanced practitioners who want to understand the theoretical foundations of human-AI collaboration at a deeper level. If the Beginner Path taught you what augmented intelligence is and the Practitioner Path taught you how to apply it, this path teaches you why it works the way it does.

For researchers, academics, and advanced practitioners seeking the theoretical foundations of human-AI collaboration. Where the Beginner Path covered what and the Practitioner Path covered how, this path covers why.
For researchers and advanced practitioners. Beginner = what; Practitioner = how; here = why.
who-2: Maybe you study how the mind works, and you want to know how AI changes the way people reason. Maybe you study how screens and tools are designed, and how that shapes what people can do. Maybe you study what makes people feel free and motivated at work. Or maybe you have been using these frameworks for a while, and now you want the deeper theory so you can go beyond the usual patterns. Any of those is a good reason to be here.

You might be a cognitive scientist interested in how AI changes the way people reason. A UX researcher studying how interface design shapes what people can do. A psychologist looking at motivation and autonomy in AI-mediated work. Or a practitioner who has used the frameworks and now wants the theoretical depth to go beyond the established patterns.

You might be a cognitive scientist interested in how AI changes human reasoning patterns. A UX researcher studying how interface design shapes augmented capability. A psychologist examining motivation and autonomy in AI-mediated work. Or a practitioner who has been applying the frameworks and now wants the theoretical depth to push beyond established patterns.

You might be a cognitive scientist studying how AI reshapes human reasoning, a UX researcher examining how interface design shapes augmented capability, a psychologist probing motivation and autonomy in AI-mediated work, or a practitioner seeking the theory to push past established patterns.
Audience: cognitive scientists (AI and reasoning), UX researchers (interface and capability), psychologists (motivation, autonomy), and practitioners wanting theory beyond established patterns.
who-3: The modules here are built on some big ideas: the theory of extended cognition, research on the limits of human thinking, self-determination theory, and the principles of good design. They will make you think hard. But they are not just theory for its own sake. They are grounded in practice, and they change the way you think about and build human-AI systems.

The modules here draw on extended cognition theory, research on the limits of human reasoning, self-determination theory, and interaction design principles. They are intellectually demanding but stay grounded in practice — theory that changes how you think about and design human-AI systems.

The modules here draw on extended cognition theory, bounded rationality research, self-determination theory, and interaction design principles. They are intellectually demanding but practically grounded — theory that changes how you think about and design human-AI systems.

The modules draw on extended cognition theory, bounded rationality research, self-determination theory, and interaction design — intellectually demanding yet practically grounded theory that reshapes how you design human-AI systems.
Drawing on extended cognition, bounded rationality, self-determination theory, and interaction design: demanding, grounded theory that reshapes how you design human-AI systems.
prereq: You need this first: Practitioner Path. This path takes for granted that you already know about meta-cognition, explanation frameworks, memory systems, trust protocols, and how to keep your practice sustainable. The theory modules here build straight on top of those hands-on ideas, so it helps to have them under your belt.

First, complete the Practitioner Path. This path assumes you are familiar with meta-cognition, explanation frameworks, memory systems, trust protocols, and sustainable practice. The theory modules build directly on those applied ideas.

Prerequisite: Practitioner Path. This path assumes familiarity with meta-cognition, explanation frameworks, memory systems, trust protocols, and sustainable practice. The theoretical modules build directly on those applied concepts.

Prerequisite: Practitioner Path. Assumes familiarity with meta-cognition, explanation frameworks, memory systems, trust protocols, and sustainable practice; the theory builds directly on those applied concepts.
Prerequisite: Practitioner Path. Assumes meta-cognition, explanation frameworks, memory systems, trust protocols, sustainable practice.
curriculum-intro: Here are the modules. Each one looks at a different part of the theory behind augmented intelligence. The first four go deep into one topic each. The fifth is a hand-picked list of books and other places to read once you want to keep going on your own.

These modules explore the theory behind augmented intelligence. The first four are deep dives into one topic each, and the fifth is a hand-picked collection of outside resources for continued research.

These modules explore the theoretical dimensions of augmented intelligence. The first four are deep dives into specific domains; the fifth is a curated collection of external resources for continued research.
These modules cover the theoretical dimensions of augmented intelligence: four deep dives into specific domains, plus a fifth curated set of external resources for continued research.
Five modules: four domain deep dives plus a curated external reading list for further research.
card-interfaces: The place where a person meets a machine is not just an empty pipe that passes things along. It shapes what you can actually do. This module looks at how the design of that meeting point sets the limit on how much AI can help you. Why does talking in plain words let you work together in ways that clicking buttons cannot? How does using your voice, typing, and pictures all together change how much your brain has to hold at once? And what has research on people and computers taught us about building tools that truly stretch what a person can do, instead of just doing tasks for them?

The interface between a person and a machine is not a neutral channel — it shapes what is possible. This module looks at how interaction design sets the ceiling of augmented intelligence: why natural language interfaces allow kinds of collaboration that button-and-menu interfaces cannot, how using several input modes changes how mental effort is shared, and what human-computer interaction research tells us about building systems that genuinely extend human capability rather than just automating tasks.

The interface between human and machine is not a neutral channel — it actively shapes what is possible. This module examines how interaction design determines the ceiling of augmented intelligence: why natural language interfaces enable certain kinds of collaboration that graphical interfaces cannot, how multi-modal input changes cognitive load distribution, and what principles from human-computer interaction research tell us about designing systems that genuinely extend human capability rather than merely automating tasks.

The human-machine interface is not neutral — it shapes what is possible. This module examines how interaction design sets the ceiling of augmented intelligence: why natural-language interfaces enable collaboration that graphical ones cannot, how multi-modal input redistributes cognitive load, and what HCI research reveals about designing systems that extend human capability rather than merely automate tasks.

The interface shapes what is possible. Examines how interaction design caps augmented intelligence: natural-language vs. graphical collaboration, multi-modal input and cognitive-load distribution, and HCI principles for extending capability over automating tasks.
card-artifacts: Cognitive artifacts are things outside your head that help you think: maps, notes, diagrams, checklists, and now the knowledge objects that AI can make. This module uses Andy Clark and David Chalmers' "extended mind" idea to show how clear thinking structures — knowledge atoms, semantic blocks, truth capsules — can become real parts of how you think, not just notes about it. You will learn how to build artifacts that do not only record your thoughts but actually join in the thinking with you.

Cognitive artifacts are external structures that take part in thinking: maps, notations, diagrams, checklists, and now AI-generated knowledge objects. This module draws on Andy Clark and David Chalmers' extended mind thesis to explore how formal thinking structures — knowledge atoms, semantic blocks, truth capsules — become real building blocks of cognition. You will learn how to design artifacts that are not just records of thought but active participants in the thinking process.

Cognitive artifacts are external structures that participate in thinking: maps, notations, diagrams, checklists, and now AI-generated knowledge objects. This module draws on Andy Clark and David Chalmers' extended mind thesis to explore how formal thinking structures — knowledge atoms, semantic blocks, truth capsules — become genuine building blocks of cognition. You will learn how to design artifacts that are not just records of thought but active participants in the thinking process itself.

Cognitive artifacts — maps, notations, diagrams, checklists, and now AI-generated knowledge objects — are external structures that participate in thinking. Drawing on Clark and Chalmers' extended mind thesis, this module shows how formal structures (knowledge atoms, semantic blocks, truth capsules) become genuine building blocks of cognition, and how to design artifacts that are active participants in thought, not just records of it.

Cognitive artifacts (maps, notations, diagrams, checklists, AI knowledge objects) participate in thinking. Via Clark and Chalmers' extended mind thesis: how formal structures — knowledge atoms, semantic blocks, truth capsules — become building blocks of cognition, and how to design artifacts that think with you rather than merely record.
card-bounded: Herbert Simon had a key idea called bounded rationality: people can only make decisions as well as the information, brainpower, and time they have at the moment allow. This idea is at the heart of why augmented intelligence matters. This module looks at how AI can stretch each of those limits — finding more information for you, taking some of the thinking load off your shoulders, and saving time by doing repetitive work. But it also looks at the dangers: new biases that AI can sneak in, leaning on the machine too much, and feeling stuck when there are simply too many choices.

Herbert Simon's idea of bounded rationality — that human decision-making is limited by the information, mental capacity, and time we have — is foundational to why augmented intelligence matters. This module explores how AI can expand each of those limits: widening available information through retrieval, extending mental capacity through offloading, and saving time through automation. It also examines the risks: new biases from AI mediation, over-reliance on machine judgment, and the paradox of choice in information-rich settings.

Herbert Simon's bounded rationality — the insight that human decision-making is limited by available information, cognitive capacity, and time — is foundational to understanding why augmented intelligence matters. This module explores how AI can expand each of those boundaries: broadening available information through retrieval, extending cognitive capacity through offloading, and compressing time through automation. But it also examines the risks: new biases introduced by AI mediation, over-reliance on machine judgment, and the paradox of choice in information-rich environments.

Herbert Simon's bounded rationality — decision-making limited by available information, cognitive capacity, and time — grounds why augmented intelligence matters. This module shows how AI expands each boundary: broadening information via retrieval, extending capacity via offloading, compressing time via automation. It also examines the risks: new AI-mediated biases, over-reliance on machine judgment, and the paradox of choice in information-rich environments.

Simon's bounded rationality — decisions limited by information, cognitive capacity, and time — grounds augmented intelligence. AI expands each: retrieval (information), offloading (capacity), automation (time). Risks: AI-mediated bias, over-reliance, paradox of choice.
card-agency: This module is built on self-determination theory, an idea from two researchers named Deci and Ryan. They found three things people need to stay engaged over time: to feel in charge of their own choices (autonomy), to feel good at what they do (competence), and to feel connected to others (relatedness). When you work with AI, these three needs can be both helped and harmed. AI can make you feel more capable, because you can do more. But it can also chip away at your sense of being in charge if you start leaning on it too much. This module shows you how to build AI workflows that protect what really keeps work meaningful — the mix of Purpose, Mastery, and Autonomy that keeps you motivated for the long haul.

Drawing on self-determination theory (from Deci and Ryan), this module looks at the three psychological needs that drive lasting engagement: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. In AI-augmented work, these needs are both served and threatened. AI can grow your sense of competence by making you more capable, but it can weaken autonomy if you become dependent on it. This module explores how to design augmented workflows that protect intrinsic motivation — the Purpose + Mastery + Autonomy triad that keeps work meaningful over the long term.

Drawing on self-determination theory (Deci and Ryan), this module examines the three psychological needs that drive sustained engagement: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. In AI-augmented work, these needs are both served and threatened. AI can increase your sense of competence by making you more capable, but it can undermine autonomy if you become dependent on it. This module explores how to design augmented workflows that preserve intrinsic motivation — the Purpose + Mastery + Autonomy triad that keeps work meaningful over the long term.

Drawing on Deci and Ryan's self-determination theory, this module examines the three needs driving sustained engagement: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. AI-augmented work both serves and threatens them — boosting competence by making you more capable, yet undermining autonomy through dependence. It covers designing workflows that preserve intrinsic motivation: the Purpose + Mastery + Autonomy triad that keeps work meaningful long-term.

Via Deci and Ryan's self-determination theory: three needs sustain engagement — autonomy, competence, relatedness. AI both serves and threatens them (raises competence, risks autonomy via dependence). Covers designing workflows that preserve the Purpose + Mastery + Autonomy triad.
card-resources: Augmented intelligence sits where several big fields meet: the science of the mind, computer science, the philosophy of how minds work, and the study of how people behave in organisations. This module gathers the most important outside books and resources, so you can keep learning well past what this site can cover on its own.

Augmented intelligence sits at the meeting point of cognitive science, computer science, philosophy of mind, and organisational psychology. This module gathers the most important outside resources for deepening your understanding beyond what this site covers.

Augmented intelligence sits at the intersection of cognitive science, computer science, philosophy of mind, and organisational psychology. This module curates the most important external resources for deepening your understanding beyond what this site covers.

Augmented intelligence sits at the intersection of cognitive science, computer science, philosophy of mind, and organisational psychology. This module curates the key external resources for deepening understanding beyond this site.
At the intersection of cognitive science, computer science, philosophy of mind, and organisational psychology — curated external resources beyond this site.
leads-1: The Researcher Path does not finish with a certificate or a final test. What it leaves you with is a question to keep turning over: is augmented intelligence just a bunch of tools and tricks, or is it a deeper change in how human thinking and machine power fit together? Sit with the modules, weigh it up, and reach your own answer — you are free to land on either side.

The Researcher Path does not end with a certificate or a final exam. What it leaves you with is a question worth testing: is augmented intelligence just a set of tools and techniques, or a deeper shift in how human thinking and machine capability relate? Work through the modules and decide for yourself.

The Researcher Path does not end with a certificate or a final exam. What it leaves you with is a question to keep testing: is augmented intelligence just a set of tools and techniques, or a deeper shift in how human cognition and machine capability relate? Work through the modules and decide for yourself.

No certificate, no exam — the Researcher Path leaves you with a question to test: is augmented intelligence just tools and techniques, or a deeper shift in how human cognition and machine capability relate? Decide for yourself.
No certificate or exam — just a question to test: is augmented intelligence merely tools and techniques, or a shift in how human cognition and machine capability relate? You decide.
leads-2: Wherever you come down on that question, the depth you have built is yours to use however you like. You might put it into research, or into building better systems, or into teaching other people, or simply into practicing augmented intelligence with more care and attention than almost anyone bothers with. What you make of it is up to you.

Wherever you land on that question, the depth is yours to use — in research, in building better systems, in teaching others, or simply in practicing augmented intelligence with a care and intention most people never reach for. What you do with it is up to you.

Wherever you land on that question, the depth is yours to use — through research, through building better systems, through teaching others, or simply through practicing augmented intelligence with a depth and intention that most people never reach for. What you do with it is your call.
Wherever you land, the depth is yours to use — research, better systems, teaching, or simply practicing augmented intelligence with a care most never reach for. What you do with it is your call.
However you land, the depth is yours — research, better systems, teaching, or practice deeper than most reach for. Your call.
highlight-goal: Knowing everything there is to know about AI isn't on the table — not for anyone. So what is all this theory actually for? One answer worth weighing: a strong base you can use to judge, adapt, and push back on the way augmented intelligence is used, even as the technology keeps changing and our understanding of it keeps growing. Whether that is reason enough to do the work is yours to decide.

Knowing everything about AI isn't on the table — for anyone. So what is deep theory for? One answer worth weighing: a foundation you can use to evaluate, adapt, and challenge augmented intelligence practices as the technology and our understanding of it keep evolving. Whether that is worth the work is yours to judge.

Knowing everything about AI isn't on the table — for anyone. So what is deep theory for? One answer worth weighing: a foundation you can use to evaluate, adapt, and challenge augmented intelligence practices as the technology and our understanding of it evolve. Whether that is worth the climb is yours to judge.

No one knows everything about AI. So what is deep theory for? Perhaps a foundation to evaluate, adapt, and challenge augmented intelligence practices as the technology and our understanding evolve — though whether that is worth it is yours to judge.
No one knows everything about AI. Deep theory's possible use: a foundation to evaluate, adapt, and challenge practices as technology and understanding evolve. Worth it? You judge.
